title: Managing Sudden Aggression Between Household Cats: A Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat suddenly acting aggressive toward housemates? Learn why this happens, when to see a vet, and how photos help triage feline conflict.
content_html:
What is Sudden Inter-Cat Aggression?

Sudden aggression between cats who previously got along—often called redirected aggression or non-recognition aggression—is a distressing behavior change. It typically occurs when a cat is startled or hyper-aroused by an external trigger (like a stray cat outside) and directs that frustration toward the nearest housemate. It can also happen after one cat returns from a veterinary visit smelling like a clinic.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for sudden aggression is Medium. While usually not an immediate life-threatening emergency, it requires prompt attention to prevent serious injury to the cats and to preserve their long-term relationship. If either cat is showing signs of physical injury, or if the aggressor is also acting lethargic or losing their appetite, a veterinary exam is needed immediately to rule out underlying pain or illness.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Providing a photo or a short video of the cats' body language can be incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. Professionals look for subtle cues like ear position, pupil dilation, and tail posture to differentiate between play, defensive fear, and offensive aggression. Seeing the physical environment where the conflict occurs also helps us suggest specific environmental modifications to restore peace.

faqs.question: Should I let my cats 'fight it out'?
faqs.answer: No. Allowing cats to fight can lead to serious injuries and permanent psychological damage to their bond. Safely separate them immediately using a barrier like a piece of cardboard to avoid being bitten yourself.

faqs.question: Can medical issues cause sudden aggression?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. Cats often hide pain. Conditions such as dental disease, arthritis, or hyperthyroidism can make a cat irritable and more likely to lash out at housemates.

faqs.question: How can I safely reintroduce my cats?
faqs.answer: Reintroduction should be a slow process involving scent swapping, visual barriers, and positive reinforcement with treats. Consult your vet for a structured behavior modification plan.
